notes.note: Imperator, the victory-title of a commander hailed by his troops, here means Caesar; Cicero jokes that what swells Trebatius's head is being asked for legal opinions by the great general himself.
Inaurari, literally 'to be gilded' --- a jibe at the money Trebatius was making in Gaul, set against the 'glory' of being consulted; Cicero wagers Trebatius prefers the prestige to the cash.

notes.anchor: lay hand on the property in due legal form
notes.note: Ex iure manum consertum: the ancient ritual of the legis actio sacramento, by which a disputant formally laid hand on contested property before the praetor. Cicero's pun is that in war-torn Gaul claims are pressed by the sword, not by Trebatius's civil-law procedure.

notes.anchor: that famous exception in the interdict
notes.note: An interdict (interdictum) was a praetor's order in property disputes; the standard exceptio unde vi excused a party who had not been the first to bring armed force. Cicero teases the timid jurist that he need not fear being the aggressor.

A powerful Belgic people of the Moselle, in open unrest against Caesar in 53 BC. The whole sentence is a pun: capitalis means both 'liable to the death penalty' and 'first-rate', and Cicero wishes the Treveri were merely the kinds of 'capital' (aere, argento, auro --- bronze, silver, gold) named in the praetor's edict.
